meta.title: Icon Veneration is CLEARLY an Accretion!
meta.author: Gavin Ortlund

questions.question: According to the speaker, what is the primary issue this video isolates—not merely religious art in churches?
options: The veneration of icons (liturgical honor, bowing, kissing, etc.)
The use of music in worship
The architectural style of basilicas
The translation of the Bible into vernacular languages
questions.answer: The veneration of icons (liturgical honor, bowing, kissing, etc.)

questions.question: Which council does Ortlund emphasize as having issued severe anathemas regarding sacred images?
options: Council of Chalcedon (451)
First Council of Nicaea (325)
Council of Trent
Second Council of Nicaea / Nicaea II (787)
questions.answer: Second Council of Nicaea / Nicaea II (787)

questions.question: Richard Price is cited for which role related to Nicaea II?
options: He was the Byzantine emperor who convened Hieria
He wrote the Catholic Catechism’s section on images
He authored the Synod of Elvira’s canons
He translated/commented on the acts of Nicaea II and summarized recent scholarly debate
questions.answer: He translated/commented on the acts of Nicaea II and summarized recent scholarly debate

questions.question: What does Ortlund claim about Basil’s statement that honor shown to an image passes to the prototype?
options: In context it concerns Trinitarian theology (e.g., emperor and coin analogy), not icon veneration
It was first coined at the Council of Frankfurt
It proves apostles venerated painted icons in the first century
It was written by Gregory the Great to Bishop Serenus
questions.answer: In context it concerns Trinitarian theology (e.g., emperor and coin analogy), not icon veneration

questions.question: Synod of Elvira, Canon 36 (early 4th century), is summarized as teaching what?
options: Icons must be kissed at every liturgy
Only Christ, not saints, may be depicted
Pictures are not to be placed in churches lest they become objects of worship and adoration
Religious art is mandatory in all new church buildings
questions.answer: Pictures are not to be placed in churches lest they become objects of worship and adoration

questions.question: In the dialogue *Octavius* by Minucius Felix, how does the Christian character respond to pagan criticism about lacking images in worship?
options: By appealing to God’s invisibility (e.g., along Deuteronomy 4 lines), not by endorsing liturgical images
By defending prostration before portraits of the saints
By arguing that icons are equal to Scripture
By claiming Christians used statues secretly
questions.answer: By appealing to God’s invisibility (e.g., along Deuteronomy 4 lines), not by endorsing liturgical images

questions.question: When Celsus attacked Christians for lacking images, how does Ortlund say Origen responded?
options: Origen denied that Christians lacked images
Origen did not dispute the lack of images and cited the second commandment
Origen converted Celsus to icon veneration
Origen argued only 3D statues were forbidden
questions.answer: Origen did not dispute the lack of images and cited the second commandment

questions.question: Epiphanius’s letter regarding a curtain with an image in a church is used to illustrate what?
options: Fourth-century indifference to images
The first legal requirement for iconostases
Irene’s political program at Nicaea II
A bishop treating images in church as contrary to Scripture and Christian religion, expecting agreement
questions.answer: A bishop treating images in church as contrary to Scripture and Christian religion, expecting agreement

questions.question: Eusebius’s letter to Constantia is cited to show what about images of Christ?
options: He rebuked the request and said such practices were excluded from churches as common knowledge
He encouraged portraits for private devotion
He invented the distinction between latria and dulia
He mandated icons in every cathedral
questions.answer: He rebuked the request and said such practices were excluded from churches as common knowledge

questions.question: Gregory the Great’s instruction to Serenus (~600) is summarized as:
options: Destroy all narrative images and ban teaching from art
Require candle-lit processions before every icon
Declare all handmade images intrinsically demonic
Preserve images for instructing the illiterate but prohibit adoration of pictorial representations
questions.answer: Preserve images for instructing the illiterate but prohibit adoration of pictorial representations

questions.question: Thomas Noble’s label for the basic Carolingian posture toward images (decoration OK; no mandated veneration) is:
options: Iconomachy
Theosis optics
Principled indifference
Pentarchic consensus
questions.answer: Principled indifference

questions.question: The Council of Hieria (754) is described as arguing primarily along which lines against images of Christ?
options: Christological dangers (e.g., separating or confusing natures) and preference for the Eucharist as Christ’s image
Arian denial of Christ’s divinity
Denial of the Old Testament canon
Support for mandatory clerical marriage
questions.answer: Christological dangers (e.g., separating or confusing natures) and preference for the Eucharist as Christ’s image

questions.question: Ortlund cites Brubaker and Haldon for which conclusion about sacred portraits?
options: Icons were ubiquitously venerated by 200 AD
Little consistent cultic veneration before the late 7th century (with rare exceptions)
Justinian banned all church art in 565
The catacombs prove mandatory prostration before images
questions.answer: Little consistent cultic veneration before the late 7th century (with rare exceptions)

questions.question: Peter Brown is associated with which scholarly point in the video?
options: The rise of the cult of icons in the 6th–7th centuries as central to understanding the controversy
Denying that Byzantium ever used images
Writing the acts of Nicaea II
Presiding at the Council of Frankfurt
questions.answer: The rise of the cult of icons in the 6th–7th centuries as central to understanding the controversy

questions.question: Ortlund’s “kiss” analogy is meant to illustrate what about worship vs. veneration distinctions for images?
options: That a later distinction can evade a simple prohibition that never admitted that distinction (like forbidding all kissing of other women)
That cultural greetings justify any kiss
That spouses should not set boundaries
That the second commandment only targets Gentile idols
questions.answer: That a later distinction can evade a simple prohibition that never admitted that distinction (like forbidding all kissing of other women)

questions.question: How does the speaker treat Joshua falling down before the ark (Joshua 7:6)?
options: As proof texts for nightly candle vigils before the ark
As normative liturgical veneration required of all Israel
As evidence that the ark was worshiped instead of God
As mourning after defeat, not ongoing cultic veneration of the ark
questions.answer: As mourning after defeat, not ongoing cultic veneration of the ark

questions.question: Hezekiah’s treatment of the bronze serpent (2 Kings 18:4) is used to show what?
options: That when veneration became a snare, destroying it was praised
That Moses sinned by making it
That Israel never used the serpent inappropriately
That the serpent proves icons of saints are apostolic
questions.answer: That when veneration became a snare, destroying it was praised

questions.question: Regarding Psalm 99:5 (“worship at his footstool”), Ortlund argues:
options: It unquestionably commands bowing *to* the ark as God
It was written by Theodulf of Orléans
Modern translations and parallelism favor “at his footstool” as place of worship, not ark-veneration; forcing the alternative mangles the text
It only applies to pagan temples
questions.answer: Modern translations and parallelism favor “at his footstool” as place of worship, not ark-veneration; forcing the alternative mangles the text

questions.question: Which empress is highlighted as responsible for convoking Nicaea II, with politically charged context noted?
options: Helena
Pulcheria
Theodora (Justinian’s consort)
Irene
questions.answer: Irene

questions.question: Ortlund claims the florilegium of patristic proof texts at Nicaea II shows what, per neutral readers like Price/Humphries?
options: Dozens of explicit fourth-century endorsements of prostrating before painted icons
Unanimous ante-Nicene support for iconodules
Relative poverty of evidence—no golden-age father explicitly approves the veneration in question
That Arnobius endorsed honor passing from image to prototype for liturgical icons
questions.answer: Relative poverty of evidence—no golden-age father explicitly approves the veneration in question

questions.question: Arnobius is cited for rejecting which anticipated defense of images?
options: That “we honor the prototype through the image” excuses praying to a lifeless likeness
That Christians may not use bread in the Eucharist
That the Septuagint is unreliable
That churches should have no roofs
questions.answer: That “we honor the prototype through the image” excuses praying to a lifeless likeness

questions.question: Augustine’s argument about sacramental vessels vs. images is used to show he opposes:
options: All use of water in baptism
Praying to consecrated vessels because they lack life/sense
Reading Scripture in Latin
The idea that a lifeless figure resembling a person can become an influential object of cultic devotion
questions.answer: The idea that a lifeless figure resembling a person can become an influential object of cultic devotion

questions.question: What approximate date does the video give for official western recognition of Nicaea II’s ecumenical status?
options: 880 AD
314 AD
754 AD
questions.answer: 880 AD

questions.question: Ortlund inverts Newman’s line (“to be deep in history…”) to claim what about icon veneration?
options: That history proves every ecumenical council was politically pure
That the early church unanimously venerated icons
That iconoclasm began in the 12th-century West only
That being deep in history on this issue pushes one toward Protestant rejection of mandated icon veneration
questions.answer: That being deep in history on this issue pushes one toward Protestant rejection of mandated icon veneration

questions.question: The speaker distinguishes kissing a Bible from icon veneration by noting that:
options: All kisses are always idolatry
The Bible may never be touched
Only Greek manuscripts may be kissed
Kisses can express affection or respect without the liturgical, mediatorial theology and anathema-backed mandates tied to icon veneration
questions.answer: Kisses can express affection or respect without the liturgical, mediatorial theology and anathema-backed mandates tied to icon veneration
